title: Modeling the Environment
subtitle: An Introduction to System Dynamics Models of Environmental Systems

authors.name: Andrew Ford
knownFor: Professor of Environmental Systems at Washington State University
Pioneer in system dynamics modeling for environmental policy
Author of the field-defining textbook Modeling the Environment
Expert in energy and climate policy modeling

description: Modeling the Environment is the definitive introduction to system dynamics modeling for environmental systems. Andrew Ford teaches readers how to build computer simulation models of environmental problems — climate change, water resources, energy policy, forest management, and urban sustainability — using the stock-and-flow feedback loop approach pioneered by Jay Forrester at MIT. The book is organized around the principle that environmental systems are fundamentally dynamic: they change over time in response to feedback, delays, and nonlinearities. Understanding these dynamics is essential for effective environmental policy. Ford introduces causal loop diagrams and stock-and-flow models as the two primary modeling tools, then applies them to a series of case studies of increasing complexity. The book emphasizes practical model building: each chapter includes model specifications, simulation results, and policy analysis. The second edition (2009) includes new material on climate change, renewable energy, and sustainability indicators. Ford's approach is distinctive for its focus on engaging stakeholders in the modeling process and using models to build consensus rather than to produce definitive predictions. The book is designed for students and professionals who want to build their own models, not just understand models built by others. It comes with a CD-ROM containing the models discussed in the text, built in the Stella and Vensim modeling environments.
keyIdeas: System dynamics models are built from stocks (accumulations like CO₂ in the atmosphere), flows (rates like emissions and absorption), and feedback loops that connect them
Causal loop diagrams reveal the feedback structure: reinforcing loops create growth or collapse, balancing loops create stability or oscillation
Delays in environmental systems — the time between a policy action and its effect — are the most common cause of policy failure, leading to overshoot and oscillation
Model validity is not about predictive accuracy but about usefulness: a model is valid if it helps stakeholders understand the system and make better decisions
Environmental models must account for nonlinearities: thresholds, tipping points, and regime shifts that cannot be captured by linear thinking
Participatory modeling — involving stakeholders in the process of building the model — increases trust, improves the model, and leads to better policy outcomes
keyTakeaways: Always begin with a causal loop diagram: draw the feedback structure before writing any equations
Identify the key stocks (what accumulates?) and flows (what changes the stocks?) before building the quantitative model
Delays are everywhere in environmental systems: the CO₂ we emit today will affect the climate for decades to centuries
Test your model under extreme conditions: if it produces absurd results for extreme inputs, fix it before using it for policy analysis
A model that is perfectly calibrated to past data may still fail to predict the future — the goal is understanding, not prediction
Share your model with stakeholders and critics; models that survive scrutiny are models that can be trusted
whoShouldRead: Environmental scientists, managers, and policy analysts who need quantitative modeling skills
Students of system dynamics, environmental science, and sustainability studies
Practitioners working on climate change, water resources, energy policy, and natural resource management
Anyone who wants to build their own simulation models of environmental systems
whoShouldSkip: Readers without basic algebra and spreadsheet skills who want a purely qualitative treatment
Those seeking a purely conceptual book about environmental systems without quantitative modeling
Mathematical modelers looking for detailed equation-level analysis of specific biological systems
excerpt: A comprehensive introduction to system dynamics modeling for environmental systems, teaching how to build simulation models of climate, energy, water, and ecological systems using feedback loops, stocks and flows, and policy analysis.
